expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to naming acids and bases, covering the systematic rules for binary acids (hydrogen + nonmetal), oxyacids (hydrogen + polyatomic ion containing oxygen), and bases (metal + hydroxide), enabling accurate communication about these important compound classes.

core_concept.principle: Acids and bases follow specific naming conventions based on their composition: binary acids use 'hydro-' prefix + '-ic acid' suffix, oxyacids use the polyatomic ion name with '-ate' → '-ic' or '-ite' → '-ous' conversion, and bases are named like ionic compounds with hydroxide.
core_concept.explanation: Acid and base nomenclature differs from other compound types because these compounds have special properties and historical naming conventions. Acids release H⁺ ions in water, so their names reflect the anion they form. Binary acids (no oxygen) use 'hydro-' prefix to distinguish them from oxyacids (with oxygen). Oxyacid names derive from their polyatomic anions: '-ate' anions become '-ic' acids, '-ite' anions become '-ous' acids. Bases contain OH⁻ and are named like ionic compounds. Understanding these patterns enables prediction of names from formulas and formulas from names for all common acids and bases.

image.alt: Flowchart showing acid and base naming decision tree
image.caption: Acid/base naming: binary acids (hydro-...-ic), oxyacids (-ate→-ic, -ite→-ous), bases (metal hydroxide).

sections.section_title: Understanding Acids and Their Naming Patterns
sections.introduction: Acids are compounds that release hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. Their naming depends on whether they contain oxygen: binary acids (no oxygen) follow one pattern, while oxyacids (with oxygen) follow another. Understanding which type you're naming is the first step in acid nomenclature.

key_concept.definition: An acid is a compound that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) in aqueous solution. Acid names indicate the anion formed when the acid dissociates: binary acids form simple anions (Cl⁻, S²⁻), while oxyacids form polyatomic oxyanions (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻).
key_concept.context: Acid formulas always start with H (written first to indicate acidic hydrogen). The naming pattern reveals whether oxygen is present and which anion is formed.

classifications.behavior: Named with 'hydro-' + nonmetal root + '-ic acid' pattern to distinguish from oxyacids.

examples.explanation: HCl in water: H⁺ + Cl⁻ (chloride). Binary acid (no oxygen). Name: hydro- + chlor + -ic acid = hydrochloric acid. The 'hydro-' indicates no oxygen present.

image.alt: HCl dissociation showing H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions
image.caption: HCl: binary acid → hydrochloric acid (hydro- + chlor + -ic acid).

examples.explanation: H₂S in water: 2H⁺ + S²⁻ (sulfide). Binary acid (no oxygen). Name: hydro- + sulf + -ic acid = hydrosulfuric acid.

image.alt: H₂S dissociation showing H⁺ and S²⁻ ions
image.caption: H₂S: binary acid → hydrosulfuric acid.

examples.explanation: HBr in water: H⁺ + Br⁻ (bromide). Binary acid. Name: hydro- + brom + -ic acid = hydrobromic acid.

image.caption: HBr: hydrobromic acid (binary acid pattern).

classifications.behavior: Named based on the polyatomic oxyanion: '-ate' → '-ic acid', '-ite' → '-ous acid'. No 'hydro-' prefix.

examples.explanation: HNO₃ in water: H⁺ + NO₃⁻ (nitrate). Oxyacid (has oxygen). Nitrate ends in '-ate' → '-ic acid'. Name: nitric acid. NO 'hydro-' prefix!

image.alt: HNO₃ dissociation showing H⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions
image.caption: HNO₃: oxyacid → nitric acid (nitrate → nitric).

examples.explanation: H₂SO₄ in water: 2H⁺ + SO₄²⁻ (sulfate). Oxyacid. Sulfate ends in '-ate' → '-ic acid'. Name: sulfuric acid.

image.alt: H₂SO₄ dissociation showing H⁺ and SO₄²⁻ ions
image.caption: H₂SO₄: sulfuric acid (sulfate → sulfuric).

examples.explanation: HNO₂ in water: H⁺ + NO₂⁻ (nitrite). Oxyacid. Nitrite ends in '-ite' → '-ous acid'. Name: nitrous acid.

image.alt: HNO₂ dissociation showing H⁺ and NO₂⁻ ions
image.caption: HNO₂: nitrous acid (nitrite → nitrous, -ite → -ous).

principles.statement: Binary acids (H + nonmetal, no oxygen) are named: hydro- + nonmetal root + -ic acid.
principles.application: HCl → hydrochloric acid (chloride → chlor + ic); H₂S → hydrosulfuric acid (sulfide → sulfur + ic); HF → hydrofluoric acid.
principles.example: HI: H⁺ + I⁻ (iodide). Binary acid. Name: hydro- + iod + -ic acid = hydroiodic acid.
principles.analogy: Like a warning label: 'hydro-' tells you 'no oxygen present'—like a 'no O₂' sign.

image.caption: Binary acid pattern: hydro- + root + -ic acid (only for acids without oxygen).

principles.statement: Oxyacids (H + polyatomic ion with oxygen) are named from the anion: '-ate' → '-ic acid', '-ite' → '-ous acid'. No 'hydro-' prefix.
principles.application: NO₃⁻ (nitrate) → HNO₃ (nitric acid); NO₂⁻ (nitrite) → HNO₂ (nitrous acid); SO₄²⁻ (sulfate) → H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid).
principles.example: ClO₃⁻ (chlorate) → HClO₃ (chloric acid); ClO₂⁻ (chlorite) → HClO₂ (chlorous acid).
principles.analogy: Like a transformation: '-ate' becomes '-ic' (both have more letters), '-ite' becomes '-ous' (both have fewer letters).

image.alt: Oxyacid naming pattern showing -ate to -ic and -ite to -ous
image.caption: Oxyacid pattern: -ate → -ic acid, -ite → -ous acid (no hydro- prefix).

principles.statement: Acid formulas always start with H (written first), followed by the anion. Subscripts balance charges to zero.
principles.application: From name to formula: identify anion charge, add H⁺ (always +1), balance charges. Hydrochloric acid → H⁺ + Cl⁻ → HCl. Sulfuric acid → H⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → H₂SO₄.
principles.example: Phosphoric acid: phosphate = PO₄³⁻, need 3 H⁺ to balance → H₃PO₄.

image.caption: Acid formulas: H written first, subscripts balance anion charge.

components.equation: $\text{Acid Name} = \begin{cases} \text{hydro-} + \text{root} + \text{-ic acid} & \text{(binary, no O)} \\ \text{root} + \text{-ic acid} & \text{(oxyacid, -ate anion)} \\ \text{root} + \text{-ous acid} & \text{(oxyacid, -ite anion)} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: Binary acids: hydro- prefix required. Oxyacids: no hydro-, name from anion (-ate→-ic, -ite→-ous). All end in 'acid'.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Acid Type and Naming
worked_examples.problem: For each acid formula, identify if it's binary or oxyacid, then name it correctly: (a) HCl, (b) HNO₃, (c) H₂S, (d) H₃PO₄, (e) HF.

step_by_step.calculation: H + Cl (no oxygen) → Binary acid → hydro- + chlor + -ic acid = hydrochloric acid
H + NO₃ (has oxygen) → Oxyacid → NO₃⁻ = nitrate (-ate) → nitric acid
H + S (no oxygen) → Binary acid → hydro- + sulfur + -ic acid = hydrosulfuric acid
H + PO₄ (has oxygen) → Oxyacid → PO₄³⁻ = phosphate (-ate) → phosphoric acid
H + F (no oxygen) → Binary acid → hydro- + fluor + -ic acid = hydrofluoric acid

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) HCl: binary → hydrochloric acid. (b) HNO₃: oxyacid (nitrate) → nitric acid. (c) H₂S: binary → hydrosulfuric acid. (d) H₃PO₄: oxyacid (phosphate) → phosphoric acid. (e) HF: binary → hydrofluoric acid. Key: check for oxygen first, then apply appropriate pattern.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying acid type (binary vs. oxyacid) and applying correct naming pattern

worked_examples.title: Distinguishing Binary from Oxyacid Patterns
worked_examples.problem: Explain why HCl is 'hydrochloric acid' but HClO₃ is 'chloric acid' (no 'hydro-'). What does the presence or absence of 'hydro-' tell you?

step_by_step.calculation: HCl: H + Cl only (no oxygen). HClO₃: H + Cl + O₃ (has oxygen).
HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻ (chloride, binary anion). HClO₃ → H⁺ + ClO₃⁻ (chlorate, oxyanion).
HCl: binary acid → hydro- + chlor + -ic = hydrochloric acid. HClO₃: oxyacid → chlorate → chlor + -ic = chloric acid (no hydro-).
'hydro-' prefix = NO oxygen present. No 'hydro-' = oxygen IS present (oxyacid).

worked_examples.final_answer: HCl is 'hydrochloric acid' (binary, no oxygen, needs hydro- prefix). HClO₃ is 'chloric acid' (oxyacid, has oxygen, no hydro- prefix). The 'hydro-' prefix specifically indicates a binary acid with NO oxygen. If you see 'hydro-' in an acid name, you know there's no oxygen. If there's no 'hydro-', the acid contains oxygen (oxyacid).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding the significance of 'hydro-' prefix in acid nomenclature

sections.introduction: Binary acids consist of hydrogen bonded to a nonmetal with no oxygen present. These acids follow a consistent naming pattern that distinguishes them from oxyacids and indicates their composition.

key_concept.definition: Binary acids contain only hydrogen and one other nonmetal element (no oxygen). They are named with the pattern: hydro- + nonmetal root + -ic acid.
key_concept.context: The 'hydro-' prefix is the key indicator that the acid contains no oxygen. This distinguishes binary acids from oxyacids which have different naming patterns.

classifications.value: Frequently encountered binary acids in chemistry
classifications.behavior: These acids dissociate completely or partially in water to release H⁺ ions.

examples.explanation: Most common binary acid. HCl(g) is hydrogen chloride gas; HCl(aq) is hydrochloric acid solution. Stomach acid contains dilute HCl. Name: hydro- + chlor + -ic acid.

image.caption: HCl: hydrochloric acid (stomach acid, industrial use).

examples.explanation: HF is unique: weak acid but highly corrosive. Etches glass (reacts with SiO₂). Name: hydro- + fluor + -ic acid. Handle with extreme care!

image.caption: HF: hydrofluoric acid (etches glass, very dangerous).

examples.explanation: H₂S is also called hydrogen sulfide (rotten egg smell). Weak acid, toxic gas. Name: hydro- + sulfur + -ic acid. Note: 'ur' kept from sulfur.

image.caption: H₂S: hydrosulfuric acid (rotten egg smell, toxic).

examples.explanation: HBr is a strong acid similar to HCl. Used in organic synthesis. Name: hydro- + brom + -ic acid.

image.caption: HBr: hydrobromic acid (strong acid, similar to HCl).

examples.explanation: HI is the strongest binary acid. Used in organic chemistry. Name: hydro- + iod + -ic acid.

image.caption: HI: hydroiodic acid (strongest binary acid).

principles.statement: To name a binary acid: (1) Confirm no oxygen in formula, (2) Identify nonmetal, (3) Add 'hydro-' prefix, (4) Use nonmetal root, (5) Add '-ic acid' suffix.
principles.application: HCl: no O ✓, Cl = chlorine, hydro- + chlor + -ic acid = hydrochloric acid. H₂S: no O ✓, S = sulfur, hydro- + sulfur + -ic acid = hydrosulfuric acid.
principles.example: HBr: Step 1: no oxygen ✓. Step 2: Br = bromine. Step 3-5: hydro- + brom + -ic acid = hydrobromic acid.

image.caption: Binary acid naming: hydro- + root + -ic acid (5 steps).

principles.statement: Some nonmetal names modify slightly when becoming acid roots: chlorine → chlor-, fluorine → fluor-, sulfur → sulfur- (keep 'ur'), bromine → brom-, iodine → iod-.
principles.application: Know these root changes: they're consistent across binary acid naming.
principles.example: Sulfur is special: keep 'ur' (hydrosulfuric, not hydrosulfic). Others drop endings: chlorine → chlor-, fluorine → fluor-.

image.alt: Table of nonmetal names and their acid roots
image.caption: Nonmetal roots: chlorine→chlor-, sulfur→sulfur- (keep ur), fluorine→fluor-.

components.interpretation: Only for acids with NO oxygen. 'hydro-' prefix is the key identifier. Nonmetal roots: chlor-, fluor-, brom-, iod-, sulfur- (keep 'ur').

worked_examples.title: Naming Binary Acids from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following binary acids: (a) HF, (b) H₂S, (c) HBr, (d) HI.

step_by_step.calculation: H + F (no oxygen) → Binary → hydro- + fluor- + -ic acid = hydrofluoric acid
H + S (no oxygen) → Binary → hydro- + sulfur- + -ic acid = hydrosulfuric acid (keep 'ur')
H + Br (no oxygen) → Binary → hydro- + brom- + -ic acid = hydrobromic acid
H + I (no oxygen) → Binary → hydro- + iod- + -ic acid = hydroiodic acid

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) HF: hydrofluoric acid. (b) H₂S: hydrosulfuric acid. (c) HBr: hydrobromic acid. (d) HI: hydroiodic acid. All follow hydro- + root + -ic acid pattern. Sulfur keeps 'ur' in the root.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming binary acids using hydro- + root + -ic acid pattern

worked_examples.title: Writing Formulas from Binary Acid Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) hydrochloric acid, (b) hydrofluoric acid, (c) hydrosulfuric acid.

step_by_step.calculation: hydro- prefix = binary (no O). 'chlor' = chlorine (Cl⁻). H⁺ + Cl⁻ → HCl
hydro- prefix = binary. 'fluor' = fluorine (F⁻). H⁺ + F⁻ → HF
hydro- prefix = binary. 'sulfur' = sulfur (S²⁻). 2H⁺ + S²⁻ → H₂S

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) HCl, (b) HF, (c) H₂S. 'hydro-' tells you it's binary (no oxygen). Root tells you the anion. Balance H⁺ (+1) with anion charge.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing binary acid formulas from names using hydro- prefix as guide

sections.introduction: Oxyacids contain hydrogen, oxygen, and another element (usually a nonmetal). Their names derive from the polyatomic oxyanion they form, following a systematic pattern based on the anion's suffix.

key_concept.definition: Oxyacids contain hydrogen, oxygen, and a central atom. They are named based on the polyatomic oxyanion: '-ate' anions become '-ic acids', '-ite' anions become '-ous acids'. No 'hydro-' prefix is used.
key_concept.context: Oxyacids are more common than binary acids and include important acids like sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄), nitric acid (HNO₃), and phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄).

examples.explanation: NO₃⁻ = nitrate (-ate). Replace -ate with -ic: nitrate → nitric acid. Important: fertilizer production, explosives.

image.caption: HNO₃: nitric acid (nitrate → nitric, -ate → -ic).

examples.explanation: SO₄²⁻ = sulfate (-ate). Replace -ate with -ic: sulfate → sulfuric acid. Most produced chemical worldwide (batteries, fertilizers).

image.caption: H₂SO₄: sulfuric acid (sulfate → sulfuric, most produced chemical).

examples.explanation: PO₄³⁻ = phosphate (-ate). Replace -ate with -ic: phosphate → phosphoric acid. Used in colas, fertilizers, rust removal.

image.caption: H₃PO₄: phosphoric acid (phosphate → phosphoric, in soft drinks).

examples.explanation: CO₃²⁻ = carbonate (-ate). Replace -ate with -ic: carbonate → carbonic acid. Forms when CO₂ dissolves in water (carbonated beverages).

image.caption: H₂CO₃: carbonic acid (carbonate → carbonic, in soda).

examples.explanation: ClO₃⁻ = chlorate (-ate). Replace -ate with -ic: chlorate → chloric acid. Strong oxidizing agent.

image.caption: HClO₃: chloric acid (chlorate → chloric).

examples.explanation: NO₂⁻ = nitrite (-ite). Replace -ite with -ous: nitrite → nitrous acid. Unstable, forms in acid rain.

image.caption: HNO₂: nitrous acid (nitrite → nitrous, -ite → -ous).

examples.explanation: SO₃²⁻ = sulfite (-ite). Replace -ite with -ous: sulfite → sulfurous acid. Forms from SO₂ in atmosphere (acid rain).

image.caption: H₂SO₃: sulfurous acid (sulfite → sulfurous, acid rain component).

examples.explanation: PO₃³⁻ = phosphite (-ite). Replace -ite with -ous: phosphite → phosphorous acid. Note spelling: phosphorous (not phosphoric).

image.caption: H₃PO₃: phosphorous acid (phosphite → phosphorous, note spelling).

examples.explanation: ClO₂⁻ = chlorite (-ite). Replace -ite with -ous: chlorite → chlorous acid. Used in bleaching.

image.caption: HClO₂: chlorous acid (chlorite → chlorous).

classifications.value: Halogens form four oxyacid series with per- and hypo- prefixes
classifications.behavior: Chlorine, bromine, iodine form four oxyacids each with different oxygen counts.

examples.explanation: ClO₄⁻ = perchlorate (most O). per-...-ate → per-...-ic acid. Strongest common acid. Name: perchloric acid.

image.caption: HClO₄: perchloric acid (perchlorate → perchloric, most oxygen).

examples.explanation: ClO⁻ = hypochlorite (least O). hypo-...-ite → hypo-...-ous acid. Active ingredient in bleach. Name: hypochlorous acid.

image.caption: HClO: hypochlorous acid (hypochlorite → hypochlorous, in bleach).

principles.statement: Remember: '-ate' has more letters → '-ic' has more letters. '-ite' has fewer letters → '-ous' has fewer letters.
principles.application: Nitrate (6 letters) → nitric (6 letters). Nitrite (6 letters) → nitrous (7 letters, but 'ous' has fewer than 'ic'). Pattern helps memorization.
principles.example: Sulfate → sulfuric (both have 'ic'). Sulfite → sulfurous (both have 'ous'). Match the suffixes.

image.caption: Mnemonic: -ate → -ic (more letters), -ite → -ous (fewer letters).

principles.statement: Oxyacids NEVER use the 'hydro-' prefix. If you see 'hydro-' in an acid name, it's definitely a binary acid (no oxygen).
principles.application: HClO₃ is chloric acid (NOT hydrochloric acid—that's HCl). H₂SO₄ is sulfuric acid (NOT hydrosulfuric acid—that's H₂S).
principles.example: Common mistake: calling HNO₃ 'hydronitric acid' (wrong!). Correct: nitric acid (no hydro- for oxyacids).

image.caption: Critical: oxyacids NEVER use hydro- prefix. hydro- = no oxygen.

principles.statement: Some oxyacid names modify the root slightly: sulfate → sulfuric (keep 'ur'), phosphate → phosphoric (add 'or'), nitrate → nitric (drop 'at').
principles.application: Know these spelling patterns: they're consistent across oxyacid naming.
principles.example: Sulfur keeps 'ur' (sulfuric, not sulfic). Phosphate adds 'or' (phosphoric, not phosphic). Nitrate drops 'at' (nitric, not nitatic).

image.caption: Spelling: sulfate→sulfuric (keep ur), phosphate→phosphoric (add or).

components.equation: $\text{Oxyacid Name} = \begin{cases} \text{Root} + \text{-ic acid} & \text{(-ate anion)} \\ \text{Root} + \text{-ous acid} & \text{(-ite anion)} \\ \text{per-} + \text{Root} + \text{-ic acid} & \text{(per-...-ate anion)} \\ \text{hypo-} + \text{Root} + \text{-ous acid} & \text{(hypo-...-ite anion)} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: No hydro- prefix ever. -ate → -ic, -ite → -ous. per- and hypo- prefixes preserved from anion name.

worked_examples.title: Naming Oxyacids from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following oxyacids: (a) H₂SO₄, (b) HNO₂, (c) HClO₃, (d) H₃PO₄, (e) HClO.

step_by_step.calculation: Has oxygen → oxyacid. Anion: SO₄²⁻ = sulfate (-ate). -ate → -ic. Name: sulfuric acid.
Has oxygen → oxyacid. Anion: NO₂⁻ = nitrite (-ite). -ite → -ous. Name: nitrous acid.
Has oxygen → oxyacid. Anion: ClO₃⁻ = chlorate (-ate). -ate → -ic. Name: chloric acid.
Has oxygen → oxyacid. Anion: PO₄³⁻ = phosphate (-ate). -ate → -ic. Name: phosphoric acid.
Has oxygen → oxyacid. Anion: ClO⁻ = hypochlorite (hypo-...-ite). hypo-...-ite → hypo-...-ous. Name: hypochlorous acid.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) H₂SO₄: sulfuric acid (sulfate → sulfuric). (b) HNO₂: nitrous acid (nitrite → nitrous). (c) HClO₃: chloric acid (chlorate → chloric). (d) H₃PO₄: phosphoric acid (phosphate → phosphoric). (e) HClO: hypochlorous acid (hypochlorite → hypochlorous). All oxyacids: no hydro- prefix, name from anion.

worked_examples.title: Writing Oxyacid Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) nitric acid, (b) sulfurous acid, (c) perchloric acid, (d) phosphoric acid.

step_by_step.calculation: -ic acid → -ate anion. Nitric → nitrate (NO₃⁻). H⁺ + NO₃⁻ → HNO₃
-ous acid → -ite anion. Sulfurous → sulfite (SO₃²⁻). 2H⁺ + SO₃²⁻ → H₂SO₃
per-...-ic → per-...-ate anion. Perchloric → perchlorate (ClO₄⁻). H⁺ + ClO₄⁻ → HClO₄
-ic acid → -ate anion. Phosphoric → phosphate (PO₄³⁻). 3H⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → H₃PO₄

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) HNO₃ (nitrate = NO₃⁻). (b) H₂SO₃ (sulfite = SO₃²⁻, need 2 H⁺). (c) HClO₄ (perchlorate = ClO₄⁻). (d) H₃PO₄ (phosphate = PO₄³⁻, need 3 H⁺). -ic = -ate anion, -ous = -ite anion, balance H⁺ with anion charge.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing oxyacid formulas from names using anion identification

sections.introduction: Bases are compounds that produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻) in aqueous solution. Their naming is simpler than acids because bases follow standard ionic compound naming rules with the hydroxide polyatomic ion.

key_concept.definition: A base is a compound that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) in aqueous solution. Bases are named like ionic compounds: metal name + hydroxide (no special acid-like patterns).
key_concept.context: Unlike acids, bases don't have special naming conventions—they're simply ionic compounds containing the OH⁻ polyatomic ion. Type I metals don't need Roman numerals; Type II metals do.

classifications.behavior: Named like Type I ionic compounds with hydroxide as the anion.

examples.explanation: Na⁺ (Group 1, fixed +1) + OH⁻ (hydroxide). Name: sodium hydroxide. Common name: lye, caustic soda. Strong base, drain cleaner.

image.caption: NaOH: sodium hydroxide (lye, strong base, drain cleaner).

examples.explanation: K⁺ (Group 1) + OH⁻. Name: potassium hydroxide. Common name: caustic potash. Strong base, soap making.

image.caption: KOH: potassium hydroxide (caustic potash, soap making).

examples.explanation: Ca²⁺ (Group 2) + OH⁻. Need 2 OH⁻ to balance. Name: calcium hydroxide. Common name: slaked lime. Used in mortar, water treatment.

image.caption: Ca(OH)₂: calcium hydroxide (slaked lime, in mortar).

examples.explanation: Al³⁺ (Group 13, fixed +3) + OH⁻. Need 3 OH⁻. Name: aluminum hydroxide. Used in antacids (neutralizes stomach acid).

image.caption: Al(OH)₃: aluminum hydroxide (antacid, neutralizes acid).

classifications.behavior: Named like Type II ionic compounds with hydroxide as the anion. Roman numeral specifies metal charge.

examples.explanation: Fe²⁺ (from name) + OH⁻. Need 2 OH⁻. Name: iron(II) hydroxide. Green precipitate, oxidizes to Fe(OH)₃ in air.

image.caption: Fe(OH)₂: iron(II) hydroxide (green precipitate).

examples.explanation: Fe³⁺ (from name) + OH⁻. Need 3 OH⁻. Name: iron(III) hydroxide. Rust-brown precipitate, common in water treatment.

image.caption: Fe(OH)₃: iron(III) hydroxide (rust-brown, water treatment).

examples.explanation: Cu²⁺ (from name) + OH⁻. Need 2 OH⁻. Name: copper(II) hydroxide. Blue precipitate, used in fungicides.

image.caption: Cu(OH)₂: copper(II) hydroxide (blue precipitate, fungicide).

classifications.behavior: NH₃(g) + H₂O(l) ⇌ NH₄⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq). Solution is basic but weak.

examples.explanation: NH₄⁺ (ammonium) + OH⁻ (hydroxide). Name: ammonium hydroxide. Actually NH₃ dissolved in water. Household ammonia cleaner.

image.caption: NH₄OH: ammonium hydroxide (ammonia in water, household cleaner).

principles.statement: Bases are named: [metal name] + hydroxide. Type I metals: no Roman numeral. Type II metals: Roman numeral required.
principles.application: NaOH: sodium hydroxide (Type I). Fe(OH)₂: iron(II) hydroxide (Type II, Fe²⁺). Ca(OH)₂: calcium hydroxide (Type I, Group 2).
principles.example: KOH: potassium hydroxide (K is Group 1). Cu(OH)₂: copper(II) hydroxide (Cu is Type II, need Roman numeral).

image.caption: Base naming: metal + hydroxide (Roman numeral for Type II metals).

principles.statement: Base formulas: metal cation + OH⁻, balance charges. Use parentheses around OH when subscript > 1.
principles.application: Na⁺ + OH⁻ → NaOH (no parentheses, 1 OH). Ca²⁺ + OH⁻ → Ca(OH)₂ (parentheses, 2 OH). Al³⁺ + OH⁻ → Al(OH)₃ (parentheses, 3 OH).
principles.example: Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₃ (need 3 OH⁻ to balance +3). Mg²⁺ + OH⁻ → Mg(OH)₂ (need 2 OH⁻ to balance +2).

image.caption: Base formulas: metal + OH⁻, parentheses when subscript > 1.

components.equation: $\text{Base Name} = \text{Metal Name} + \text{hydroxide} \quad \text{(Roman numeral if Type II)}$
components.interpretation: Bases follow ionic naming rules. OH⁻ is the polyatomic anion. Parentheses in formula when more than 1 OH⁻ needed.

worked_examples.title: Naming Bases from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following bases: (a) NaOH, (b) Ca(OH)₂, (c) Fe(OH)₃, (d) Cu(OH)₂, (e) Al(OH)₃.

step_by_step.calculation: Na⁺ (Group 1, Type I) + OH⁻ → sodium hydroxide (no Roman numeral)
Ca²⁺ (Group 2, Type I) + 2OH⁻ → calcium hydroxide (no Roman numeral)
3 OH⁻ = -3, Fe must = +3. Fe is Type II → iron(III) hydroxide
2 OH⁻ = -2, Cu must = +2. Cu is Type II → copper(II) hydroxide
Al³⁺ (Group 13, Type I) + 3OH⁻ → aluminum hydroxide (no Roman numeral)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) NaOH: sodium hydroxide. (b) Ca(OH)₂: calcium hydroxide. (c) Fe(OH)₃: iron(III) hydroxide. (d) Cu(OH)₂: copper(II) hydroxide. (e) Al(OH)₃: aluminum hydroxide. Type I metals (Groups 1, 2, Al) don't need Roman numerals; Type II (transition) do.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming bases using ionic compound rules with hydroxide ion

worked_examples.title: Writing Base Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) potassium hydroxide, (b) magnesium hydroxide, (c) iron(II) hydroxide, (d) chromium(III) hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: K⁺ (Group 1) + OH⁻ → 1:1 ratio → KOH (no parentheses needed)
Mg²⁺ (Group 2) + OH⁻ → Need 2 OH⁻ → Mg(OH)₂ (parentheses needed)
Fe²⁺ (from II) + OH⁻ → Need 2 OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂ (parentheses needed)
Cr³⁺ (from III) + OH⁻ → Need 3 OH⁻ → Cr(OH)₃ (parentheses needed)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) KOH, (b) Mg(OH)₂, (c) Fe(OH)₂, (d) Cr(OH)₃. Parentheses around OH when subscript > 1. Metal charge determines how many OH⁻ needed (OH⁻ is always -1).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing base formulas using metal charge and hydroxide ion

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Acid and base nomenclature has specific rules that differ from other compound types. Recognizing common pitfalls and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and prevents confusion.

key_concept.definition: Common acid/base naming errors include: using 'hydro-' for oxyacids, forgetting 'hydro-' for binary acids, mixing up -ic/-ous suffixes, omitting Roman numerals for Type II bases, and incorrect parentheses in base formulas.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: binary or oxyacid? hydro- used correctly? -ic/-ous matches anion? Base has hydroxide? Roman numeral for Type II? Parentheses when needed?

classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect names; 'hydro-' is ONLY for binary acids (no oxygen).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'hydronitric acid' for HNO₃. Correct: 'nitric acid'. HNO₃ has oxygen (oxyacid), so NO hydro- prefix.

image.alt: Comparison showing incorrect vs. correct hydro- usage
image.caption: Acid error: never use hydro- for oxyacids (acids with oxygen).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'hydrosulfuric acid' for H₂SO₄. Correct: 'sulfuric acid'. H₂SO₄ has oxygen. 'Hydrosulfuric acid' is actually H₂S (no oxygen).

image.caption: H₂SO₄ = sulfuric acid (oxyacid); H₂S = hydrosulfuric acid (binary).

classifications.behavior: Results in wrong compound identification: 'chloric acid' is HClO₃ (oxyacid), not HCl (binary).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'chloric acid' for HCl. Correct: 'hydrochloric acid'. HCl has no oxygen (binary), needs hydro- prefix.

image.caption: HCl: hydrochloric acid (binary, needs hydro-); HClO₃: chloric acid (oxyacid, no hydro-).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'sulfuric acid' for H₂S. Correct: 'hydrosulfuric acid'. H₂S has no oxygen. 'Sulfuric acid' is H₂SO₄.

image.caption: H₂S: hydrosulfuric acid (binary); H₂SO₄: sulfuric acid (oxyacid).

classifications.value: Using wrong suffix for oxyacid based on anion
classifications.behavior: Results in wrong compound: nitric acid (HNO₃) and nitrous acid (HNO₂) are different compounds with different properties.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'nitrous acid' for HNO₃. Correct: 'nitric acid'. NO₃⁻ = nitrate (-ate) → -ic acid, not -ous.

image.caption: HNO₃: nitric acid (nitrate, -ate→-ic); HNO₂: nitrous acid (nitrite, -ite→-ous).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'sulfuric acid' for H₂SO₃. Correct: 'sulfurous acid'. SO₃²⁻ = sulfite (-ite) → -ous acid, not -ic.

image.caption: H₂SO₄: sulfuric acid (sulfate, -ate); H₂SO₃: sulfurous acid (sulfite, -ite).

classifications.value: Forgetting parentheses around hydroxide when subscript > 1
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect formulas: CaOH₂ suggests 1 Ca, 1 O, 2 H (wrong); Ca(OH)₂ correctly shows 1 Ca, 2 O, 2 H.

examples.process: Error: missing parentheses for calcium hydroxide
examples.explanation: Incorrect: CaOH₂ for calcium hydroxide. Correct: Ca(OH)₂. Parentheses show subscript 2 applies to entire OH unit, not just H.

image.alt: Comparison showing correct vs. incorrect parentheses usage
image.caption: Base formula error: Ca(OH)₂ not CaOH₂; parentheses show 2 complete hydroxide ions.

examples.process: Error: missing parentheses for iron(III) hydroxide
examples.explanation: Incorrect: FeOH₃ for iron(III) hydroxide. Correct: Fe(OH)₃. Without parentheses, OH₃ suggests 1 O and 3 H, not 3 OH⁻ ions.

image.caption: Fe(OH)₃ not FeOH₃; parentheses show 3 complete hydroxide ions.

principles.statement: Always verify acid/base names by checking: (1) Acid type identified correctly (binary vs. oxyacid), (2) hydro- used only for binary acids, (3) -ic/-ous matches -ate/-ite anion, (4) Base has hydroxide, (5) Roman numeral for Type II base metals, (6) Parentheses in base formulas when subscript > 1.
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit.
principles.example: Name: sulfuric acid. Check: oxyacid (has O) ✓, no hydro- ✓, sulfate (-ate) → -ic ✓. Name: calcium hydroxide. Check: base (has OH) ✓, Ca is Type I (no Roman numeral) ✓, Ca(OH)₂ (parentheses for 2 OH) ✓.

image.caption: Acid/base checklist: acid type, hydro- correct, -ic/-ous match, base has OH, Roman numeral, parentheses.

principles.statement: First question when naming an acid: Does the formula contain oxygen? Yes → oxyacid (no hydro-). No → binary acid (use hydro-).
principles.application: This single question prevents the most common acid naming error (using hydro- incorrectly).
principles.example: HCl: has O? No → binary → hydrochloric acid (hydro-). HClO₃: has O? Yes → oxyacid → chloric acid (no hydro-).

image.caption: Acid naming decision: has oxygen? Yes→oxyacid (no hydro-), No→binary (use hydro-).

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each name or formula and provide the correct version: (a) 'hydronitric acid' for HNO₃, (b) 'chloric acid' for HCl, (c) 'nitrous acid' for HNO₃, (d) CaOH₂ for calcium hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: Error: hydro- used for oxyacid. HNO₃ has oxygen. Correction: NO₃⁻ = nitrate (-ate) → nitric acid (no hydro-).
Error: no hydro- for binary acid. HCl has no oxygen. Correction: HCl is binary → hydrochloric acid (needs hydro-).
Error: wrong suffix. HNO₃ has NO₃⁻ (nitrate, -ate). Correction: -ate → -ic → nitric acid (not nitrous).
Error: missing parentheses. Ca²⁺ needs 2 OH⁻. Correction: Ca(OH)₂ (parentheses show 2 complete OH⁻ ions).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 'hydronitric acid' → nitric acid (remove hydro-; HNO₃ is oxyacid). (b) 'chloric acid' → hydrochloric acid (add hydro-; HCl is binary). (c) 'nitrous acid' → nitric acid (change -ous to -ic; nitrate → nitric). (d) CaOH₂ → Ca(OH)₂ (add parentheses for 2 OH⁻). Systematic verification catches these common acid/base errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction using acid/base nomenclature verification checklist

sections.section_title: Practice: Mastering Acid and Base Nomenclature
sections.introduction: Acid and base nomenclature mastery requires active practice with identification, naming, and formula writing. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: identifying acid type (binary vs. oxyacid), naming acids and bases from formulas, writing formulas from names, and correcting common errors.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce pattern recognition; focus on hydro- usage, -ic/-ous suffixes, and base parentheses.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Acid and Base Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Complete the following: (1-5) Name these compounds: HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₂, NaOH, Fe(OH)₃. (6-10) Write formulas for: hydrofluoric acid, phosphoric acid, sulfurous acid, potassium hydroxide, copper(II) hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: H + Cl (no O) → Binary → hydro- + chlor + -ic acid = hydrochloric acid
H + SO₄ (has O) → Oxyacid → sulfate (-ate) → sulfuric acid
H + NO₂ (has O) → Oxyacid → nitrite (-ite) → nitrous acid
Na⁺ (Type I) + OH⁻ → sodium hydroxide (no Roman numeral)
3 OH⁻ = -3, Fe = +3, Fe is Type II → iron(III) hydroxide
hydro- = binary (no O). Fluor = F⁻. H⁺ + F⁻ → HF
-ic = -ate anion. Phosphoric → phosphate (PO₄³⁻). 3H⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → H₃PO₄
-ous = -ite anion. Sulfurous → sulfite (SO₃²⁻). 2H⁺ + SO₃²⁻ → H₂SO₃
K⁺ + OH⁻ → 1:1 ratio → KOH (no parentheses)
Cu²⁺ + OH⁻ → Need 2 OH⁻ → Cu(OH)₂ (parentheses needed)

worked_examples.final_answer: Names: (1) hydrochloric acid, (2) sulfuric acid, (3) nitrous acid, (4) sodium hydroxide, (5) iron(III) hydroxide. Formulas: (6) HF, (7) H₃PO₄, (8) H₂SO₃, (9) KOH, (10) Cu(OH)₂. Binary acids use hydro-; oxyacids don't. -ate→-ic, -ite→-ous. Bases: metal + hydroxide, parentheses when >1 OH.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive acid and base nomenclature practice covering naming and formula writing

worked_examples.title: Acid Type Identification Challenge
worked_examples.problem: For each acid, identify if binary or oxyacid, then name correctly: (a) HBr, (b) HClO₄, (c) H₂S, (d) H₃PO₃, (e) HI, (f) HClO.

step_by_step.calculation: H + Br (no O) → Binary → hydrobromic acid
H + ClO₄ (has O) → Oxyacid → perchlorate (-ate) → perchloric acid
H + S (no O) → Binary → hydrosulfuric acid
H + PO₃ (has O) → Oxyacid → phosphite (-ite) → phosphorous acid
H + I (no O) → Binary → hydroiodic acid
H + ClO (has O) → Oxyacid → hypochlorite (hypo-...-ite) → hypochlorous acid

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) HBr: binary → hydrobromic acid. (b) HClO₄: oxyacid (perchlorate) → perchloric acid. (c) H₂S: binary → hydrosulfuric acid. (d) H₃PO₃: oxyacid (phosphite) → phosphorous acid. (e) HI: binary → hydroiodic acid. (f) HClO: oxyacid (hypochlorite) → hypochlorous acid. Key: check for oxygen first, then apply correct pattern.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying acid type and applying correct naming pattern

summary: Acids and bases follow specific naming conventions. Binary acids (H + nonmetal, no oxygen) use hydro- + root + -ic acid pattern: HCl = hydrochloric acid, H₂S = hydrosulfuric acid. Oxyacids (H + polyatomic ion with oxygen) use anion-based naming: -ate → -ic acid (NO₃⁻ nitrate → HNO₃ nitric acid), -ite → -ous acid (NO₂⁻ nitrite → HNO₂ nitrous acid), per-...-ate → per-...-ic acid (ClO₄⁻ perchlorate → HClO₄ perchloric acid), hypo-...-ite → hypo-...-ous acid (ClO⁻ hypochlorite → HClO hypochlorous acid). Critical: oxyacids NEVER use hydro- prefix; hydro- means NO oxygen. Bases are named like ionic compounds: metal + hydroxide. Type I metals (Groups 1, 2, Al): no Roman numeral (NaOH = sodium hydroxide). Type II metals (transition): Roman numeral required (Fe(OH)₃ = iron(III) hydroxide). Base formulas use parentheses around OH when subscript > 1: Ca(OH)₂ not CaOH₂. Common errors: using hydro- for oxyacids (wrong), omitting hydro- for binary acids (wrong), mixing -ic/-ous suffixes (wrong), missing parentheses in base formulas (wrong). Verification checklist: acid type correct? hydro- used correctly? -ic/-ous matches anion? Base has OH? Roman numeral for Type II? Parentheses when needed? Mastery of acid/base nomenclature essential for all future chemistry.
